title: STUDIO Science — Why the Sky Is Blue

warnings: Unknown channel 'educational' — valid: social, streaming, theatrical, festival, client
[SCIENCE] illustrative-not-simulation disclosure required — state visuals are educational illustration, not simulation output
notes: Row 6: AI-disclosure obligation planned — verify presence at Pre-Publish.
Social = mass dissemination. Platform AI-label + community guidelines apply.

science_subject.key_measurement: Rayleigh scattering intensity scales as 1/λ⁴ — shorter wavelengths scatter more
science_subject.visualization_prompt: Scientific visualization: sunlight entering Earth's atmosphere, blue shorter wavelengths scattering in all directions while red passes through at sunset angle; accurate color, neutral clinical grade, no decorative bloom

sources.citation: Lord Rayleigh, "On the scattering of light by small particles," Philosophical Magazine, 1871
NASA Earth Observatory — Why Is the Sky Blue?
Craig F. Bohren & Donald R. Huffman, Absorption and Scattering of Light by Small Particles (Wiley, 1983)

config.voice_suffix: clear enthusiastic science communicator voice, precise but accessible diction, synthetic presenter only

shots.video_prompt: CONTINUITY LOCK @Julian-001: identical presenter face, smart-casual wardrobe, seamless neutral set, same eye-line to camera — seamless continuation of prior take, zero jump cut. CONTINUITY LOCK @Set-Seamless-Neutral-001: identical neutral grey seamless — same backdrop tone mid-grey #9A9A98, same floor sweep, same subject-to-backdrop distance, same falloff — seamless continuation, zero jump cut. STYLE LOCK @Style-Cool-Clinical-001: cool-clinical grade — neutral white balance, low saturation, accurate color reproduction, minimal stylistic grading, science and product trust, no warm cozy cast, no film grain. Presenter addresses camera on seamless neutral, crisp science hook. Camera: locked medium, direct address. Neutral seamless 5500K bilateral soft key locked — symmetrical wrap, subtle nose shadow only, zero hue drift, no magenta vignette, no warm/cool lateral split. Neutral 5600–6500K even field — shadow definition for shape only, no gel, no warm practicals in frame. COLOR LOCK Seamless-Neutral: backdrop #9A9A98±2%, skin true-to-life, wardrobe colors accurate, no gel spill — no magenta edge gradient, no orange spotlight cone. GRADE LOCK Cool-Clinical: grey axis neutral, skin neither pink nor yellow pushed, saturation -15% global — forbid magenta tint, forbid orange warmth, forbid teal skin on shadows, forbid vintage fade. Locked-off or slow linear track; no handheld. Lip-synced, delivers: "Look up on a clear day — the sky is blue. But sunlight is white. So where did the blue go?" clear enthusiastic science communicator voice, precise but accessible diction, synthetic presenter only. Finish on presentational gesture peak, never hold dead stillness or dead frame.
shots.speech_text: Look up on a clear day — the sky is blue. But sunlight is white. So where did the blue go?

shots.speech_text: Sunlight is all colors traveling together. Air molecules are tiny — smaller than the wavelength of visible light — so they scatter short blue waves in every direction.

shots.speech_text: Lord Rayleigh worked out the math in 1871: scatter intensity rises sharply as wavelength shrinks — roughly one over lambda to the fourth power.

shots.speech_text: Watch the payoff: blue light ricochets through the atmosphere toward your eyes from every direction — while redder light sails through at low sun angles, painting sunsets.
shots.visualization_prompt: Rayleigh scattering diagram: white sunbeam entering atmosphere, blue photons scattering omnidirectionally, red-orange beam passing through at grazing angle; clinical accurate color

shots.speech_text: One simple scattering law explains the color above you, the blush at dusk, and why distant mountains look hazy blue. Physics hiding in plain sight.
